name: Chunking: Group Information into Digestible Units
trigger: A screen presents a long sequence of items, a lengthy form, a string of digits, or dense content that the user must read, hold in mind, or input.
recommendation: Group related information into a small number of meaningful chunks rather than one long undifferentiated stream: format long numbers in groups (e.g. card numbers in 4-4-4-4), section long forms under clear headings, cluster navigation into labelled categories, and use whitespace and headings to create scannable units. Chunking reduces working-memory load and error.
rejection: Reject unbroken 30-field forms, unformatted long numeric inputs, flat lists of dozens of links with no grouping, and walls of text with no headings or sections.
validation: Check long inputs and content are segmented into labelled or formatted chunks
Verify forms group fields by topic under headings
Confirm no single ungrouped list forces users to hold many items in memory at once
evidence: Miller, G.A. (1956) 'The Magical Number Seven, Plus or Minus Two' on chunking and short-term memory
Note: the '7±2' figure is about chunk capacity for recall, not a hard limit on menu items; the durable lesson is chunking, not a magic number
